chunk_summary: Guidance on vitamin D unit conversion, repletion course design, and reassessment in pediatric CKD, emphasizing safety, monitoring, and separation of native and active vitamin D therapies.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of unit consistency in vitamin D measurement and conversion in pediatric CKD.
Learn how to design and monitor a finite vitamin D repletion course based on severity and reassessment timelines.
Differentiate between native vitamin D repletion and active vitamin D analogue therapy in CKD-MBD management.
Recognize the risks of hypercalcemia and adynamic bone disease during vitamin D therapy.
key_facts: Serum 25(OH)D is reported as ng/mL or nmol/L; use the conversion: nmol/L = 2.496 × (ng/mL).
Repletion should target <30 ng/mL (approximately 75 nmol/L) and be reassessed after 8–12 weeks.
Monitor calcium, phosphate, and PTH trends during repletion to avoid hypercalcemia.
Do not use calcitriol/alfacalcidol to treat low 25(OH)D; reserve active analogues for CKD-MBD with persistent PTH elevation.
Document stopping rules (e.g., target reached, hypercalcemia) and next lab date to ensure continuity of care.
tags_mesh: Chronic Kidney Disease-Mineral and Bone Disorder
reference_quote: Do not use calcitriol/alfacalcidol to treat low 25(OH)D; active analogues are reserved for CKD–mineral bone disorder when PTH remains persistently above stage-appropriate targets despite correction of modifiable factors (phosphate control, calcium balance, and native vitamin D status).
document_key_topics: Vitamin D unit conversion in CKD
Separation of native and active vitamin D therapies
Monitoring for hypercalcemia and CKD-MBD risks
